failure_surface: Row index offset error caused out-of-bounds writes exceeding source worksheet dimensions (wrote to row 19 when worksheet has only 18 rows)
root_cause: Agent miscounted the relationship between DataFrame row indices and target Excel row numbers when writing multi-row data ranges
minimal_fix_hypothesis: Before writing to Excel, verify that calculated target row numbers do not exceed the source worksheet's maximum row count, and adjust start/end row calculations accordingly
evidence: Foglio1 shape reported as (18, 16) meaning rows indexed 0-17 in Python = rows 1-18 in Excel
Loop iterate over all 18 result_df rows and assigns excel_row = row_idx + 2
For row_idx=17, excel_row becomes 19, which exceeds the 18-row limit
Verification log shows 'G19 = 2-1' confirming write to non-existent row 19
Code comment states 'starting from row 2' without accounting for whether existing data fits target range

memory_items.title: Validate Target Range Before Bulk Write
memory_items.lesson: Always verify that calculated target cell addresses fall within valid worksheet boundaries before writing multiple rows/columns
memory_items.generalization: Calculate max_target_row = start_row + len(data_to_write) - 1 and compare against wb.max_row before writing

memory_items.title: Account for Header Rows in Index Translation
memory_items.lesson: When writing starting after a header row, the Excel row offset depends on whether target rows already exist or are new content area
memory_items.generalization: Distinguish between 'overwrite existing content range' vs 'append to end' scenarios; use consistent 1-indexed arithmetic

memory_items.title: Handle Mixed-Type Column Data During Sorting/Comparison
memory_items.lesson: Columns may contain mixed integer and string types requiring normalization before sorting or set operations
memory_items.generalization: Apply type coercion (e.g., str()) to all comparison values when column dtype is object with heterogeneous contents

edits.content: **Range Validation**: Before bulk-writing to the target range, calculate the expected final row number (start_row + data_rows - 1) and ensure it does not exceed the worksheet's physical row limit (wb.max_row). If the target range extends beyond existing rows, consider appending or extending columns rather than overwriting.
